title: LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation
concept: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) factorizes the weight update matrix into two low-rank matrices (A and B), updating only these small layers during training to save memory.

exercises.prompt: What does the acronym LoRA stand for?
exercises.explain: LoRA stands for Low-Rank Adaptation, a technique designed to make adapting pre-trained models highly efficient.

cards.title: AI Training on a Budget
cards.body: Training giant AI models usually requires supercomputers that cost millions of dollars. But what if you could adapt a massive AI to do a new task on a single, affordable gaming computer? That is the magic of LoRA!

cards.title: What is LoRA?
cards.body: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) is a smart way to fine-tune AI. Instead of changing millions of parameters in a giant weight matrix, it splits the update into two much smaller, narrow matrices (called A and B). We only train these tiny matrices, keeping the main model completely frozen.

cards.title: The Sticky Note Trick
cards.body: Imagine your neural network is a giant heavy textbook. Instead of rewriting and republishing the entire 1,000-page book to add a small new chapter (traditional fine-tuning), you just write a tiny sticky note (LoRA) and paste it on the side. The book stays exactly the same, but you get the updated information quickly and with almost zero extra weight!

cards.title: LoRA in Action
cards.body: Imagine you want to teach a massive medical AI how to chat specifically about dentistry. Instead of retraining all 70 billion parameters, you attach a tiny LoRA layer. The LoRA layer learns the dentistry-specific terms using a fraction of the hardware, saving you thousands of dollars in electricity and computing costs.

cards.title: Is Smaller Worse?
cards.body: Many think that because LoRA uses tiny matrices, the AI's final performance must be much worse than full training. In reality, LoRA often achieves almost identical accuracy to full fine-tuning, while using up to 10,000 times fewer trainable parameters!

cards.title: Full Tuning vs. LoRA
cards.body: See how LoRA compares to traditional full fine-tuning when adapting a model to a new task.

left.text: Updates every single parameter in the giant model. Requires massive supercomputer memory.

right.text: Freezes the giant model. Only updates two tiny, narrow side matrices. Fits on a standard GPU.

exercises.prompt: How does LoRA mathematically reduce the size of the updates we need to train?

options.text: By breaking the update matrix into two smaller matrices
By deleting half of the model's layers
By converting all numbers in the AI into text
By training only on tiny, low-quality images

exercises.explain: LoRA factorizes the weight update matrix into two smaller, low-rank matrices (A and B). This math trick vastly reduces the number of values we need to calculate and update.

exercises.prompt: True or False: LoRA allows you to fine-tune large AI models using significantly less computer memory (VRAM).
exercises.explain: This is true. Because the main weight matrix is frozen, we do not need to calculate gradients or optimizer states for it, resulting in massive hardware memory savings.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe how the original weights behave during LoRA training.
exercises.sentence: During LoRA training, the original massive weight matrix remains completely [blank].
exercises.explain: By keeping the original weights frozen, we don't have to calculate or store updates for them, which saves massive amounts of computer memory.

exercises.prompt: Which of these shows an AI making a fundamental mistake about how LoRA works?

options.text: An engineer freezing the base model and training only the low-rank adapters A and B.
An AI assistant writing a script that updates all 175 billion base parameters while claiming it is 'running a fast LoRA training loop'.
A developer merging the trained small matrices back into the main weights for fast inference.

exercises.explain: Option B shows an AI attempting to write code for LoRA but getting it completely wrong by trying to train the base model weights, which defeats the purpose of LoRA.

exercises.prompt: How does LoRA manage to update a massive AI model while using barely any computer memory?

options.text: It replaces the original weights entirely with random numbers
It makes the model run slower during final deployment
It trains only the small factorized matrices and merges them later
It requires double the computer memory of full fine-tuning

exercises.explain: LoRA freezes the original massive weight matrix and only trains the two smaller matrices, A and B. During inference, these are added back to the original weights, meaning you get the benefits of adaptation with zero extra delay!
